title: The quiet violence of being unrecognised at work
summary: Name a harm workplaces inflict without breaking any rule, and ask what real recognition would actually require.
topic: Being unrecognised at work — the harm and the remedy
prompt: Write a 280–360-word argumentative essay naming the harm of being chronically unrecognised at work, explaining why our vocabulary fails to name it, and proposing what real recognition would actually look like. Use hedging and nuanced register. End on a sentence that names the cost of changing this, not just the benefit.
hintStarters: There is a particular kind of harm that workplaces inflict without…
We are not very good at naming this harm, because…
What might recognition look like, if it were taken seriously as…
These are not large changes. The reason they are rare is not that…
sampleText:
There is a particular kind of harm that workplaces inflict without any rule being broken, any voice being raised, or any single moment that could later be pointed at. It is the harm of being present in a room for years and never quite being noticed in it. The accountant whose suggestions are repeated, two minutes later, by a more senior colleague and treated as fresh. The cleaner whose name no one asks. The junior researcher whose footnote becomes the headline of someone else's paper. None of these are scandals, individually. Each is a small inaudible click that, when accumulated over a career, becomes a kind of low-grade tinnitus the body never forgets.

We are not very good at naming this harm, because our vocabulary of workplace injury was built around louder events: harassment, fraud, dismissal. The quiet kind has no police report attached to it. It is also disturbingly easy for the people doing the unrecognising to feel that nothing has happened — and, in their universe, nothing has. The injury exists only in the universe of the unrecognised, who, after enough of it, often stop describing it even to themselves.

What might recognition look like, if it were taken seriously as an obligation rather than a personality trait? It would, I think, look less like grand performance and more like minor and stubborn precision. The right person credited in the right meeting, not afterwards. A second sentence after "thank you" that names what was thanked. A promotion process that requires evidence of who was first, not merely who spoke last.

These are not large changes. The reason they are rare is not that they are difficult. It is that they require giving up something — the comfortable warmth of mild, lifelong appropriation — that very few institutions have ever been asked to surrender.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: quấy rối, gian lận, sa thải
những người đang không công nhận
những người không được công nhận
sự chính xác nhỏ nhưng bền bỉ
sự chiếm đoạt nhẹ nhàng và kéo dài cả đời

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hypothetical 'if it were' for proposals
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What might recognition look like, if it were taken seriously as an obligation…' dùng cấu trúc giả định để đề xuất một khả năng nghiêm túc.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What might recognition look like, if it were taken seriously as an obligation…' uses a hypothetical structure to propose a serious possibility.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to name a hidden cost
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The reason they are rare is not that they are difficult. It is that they require giving up something…' dùng cấu trúc chẻ để bộc lộ điều thực sự khó: phải từ bỏ một đặc quyền.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The reason they are rare is not that they are difficult. It is that they require giving up something…' uses a cleft to expose the real difficulty: surrendering a privilege.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một dạng tổn thương mà nơi làm việc gây ra mà không phá vỡ quy tắc nào, không có tiếng la nào, không có khoảnh khắc đơn nhất nào có thể chỉ ra sau này. Đó là nỗi đau của việc có mặt trong một căn phòng suốt nhiều năm mà chưa bao giờ thật sự được nhận ra trong nó. Người kế toán có những đề xuất được lặp lại, hai phút sau, bởi một đồng nghiệp cấp cao hơn và được coi như mới. Người lao công không ai hỏi tên. Nghiên cứu sinh trẻ có chú thích trở thành tựa đề bài báo của người khác. Từng việc một không phải là vụ bê bối. Mỗi việc là một tiếng tách rất nhỏ, khi tích tụ qua một sự nghiệp, trở thành một dạng ù tai nhẹ mà cơ thể không bao giờ quên.

Chúng ta không giỏi gọi tên dạng tổn thương này, vì vốn từ về thương tích nơi công sở được xây quanh những sự việc ồn ào hơn: quấy rối, gian lận, sa thải. Dạng âm thầm không kèm bản báo cáo cảnh sát nào. Nó cũng dễ một cách đáng lo ngại để những người đang không công nhận cảm thấy rằng không có gì xảy ra — và, trong vũ trụ của họ, không có gì xảy ra thật. Tổn thương chỉ tồn tại trong vũ trụ của những người không được công nhận, những người, sau đủ lâu, thường ngừng mô tả nó ngay cả với chính mình.

Sự công nhận sẽ trông ra sao, nếu được xem nghiêm túc như một nghĩa vụ chứ không phải một đức tính cá nhân? Nó, tôi nghĩ, sẽ ít giống biểu diễn long trọng và giống nhiều hơn với sự chính xác nhỏ nhưng bền bỉ. Đúng người được ghi công trong đúng cuộc họp, không phải sau đó. Một câu thứ hai sau "cảm ơn" gọi tên điều được cảm ơn. Một quy trình thăng chức đòi hỏi bằng chứng về ai là người đầu tiên, không phải ai là người nói cuối.

Đây không phải những thay đổi lớn. Lý do chúng hiếm gặp không phải vì chúng khó. Mà vì chúng đòi hỏi từ bỏ một thứ — sự ấm áp dễ chịu của một sự chiếm đoạt nhẹ và kéo dài cả đời — mà rất ít tổ chức từng được yêu cầu phải nhường lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a hypothetical 'if it were' construction correctly at C1?
options: What might recognition look like, if it were taken seriously as an obligation rather than a personality trait?
What recognition look like, if it taken serious as obligation, not personality?
What recognition is being like, if it was taking serious obligation?
Recognition look serious how, was if it taken obligation rather trait.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'if it were taken seriously' = hypothetical past subjunctive in a passive construction, paired with 'might'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'might + verb' paired with 'if it were + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to expose the real cause of inaction?
options: The reason they are rare is not that they are difficult. It is that they require giving up something.
They are rare and difficult, but giving up is not it.
Difficult they are not, give up they are it real reason.
Reason them rare hard not — surrender something is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The reason X is not Y. It is Z' is a paired cleft that delays and reveals the real cause.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is X that/because' pattern used as a reveal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses three parallel relative clauses to give weight to a list?
options: The accountant whose suggestions are repeated by a senior colleague. The cleaner whose name no one asks. The junior researcher whose footnote becomes the headline of someone else's paper.
Accountant their suggestions someone takes. Cleaner name no asking. Junior footnote becomes paper headline.
Senior colleague repeats accountant. No asks cleaner name. Junior footnote that paper.
Whose accountant suggestions, whose cleaner name, whose junior footnote — these are.
mcQuestions.explanation: Three full noun-phrase fragments, each opened with 'whose + noun', create rhetorical weight without becoming a list.
mcQuestions.hint: Three sentences in a row, each opening with 'The [role] whose …', is the standard parallel pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing sentence names what changing this would cost, instead of just promising a benefit?
options: These changes would make everyone happier and more productive at work.
In conclusion, recognition is something every workplace should provide.
It is that they require giving up something — the comfortable warmth of mild, lifelong appropriation — that very few institutions have ever been asked to surrender.
Throughout history, recognition has always been important to humans.
mcQuestions.explanation: C names the structural privilege institutions would have to give up — a real cost, not a vague promise.
mcQuestions.hint: A serious closing names what must be *surrendered*, not just gained.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the most controlled C1 register, neither slangy nor stiff?
options: It is also disturbingly easy for the people doing the unrecognising to feel that nothing has happened — and, in their universe, nothing has.
It's super easy for these unrecognising guys to feel like nothing happened.
It is, accordingly and notwithstanding, easy for unrecognisers feeling nothing.
These people feel nothing because they unrecognise so much.
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps a controlled, slightly literary register with one strong adverb ('disturbingly') and an embedded clause; the others are too casual, too jargony, or too plain.
mcQuestions.hint: C1 register is precise without being slangy or pompous.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of one person at your workplace who is often unrecognised. What is one small, structural change (not a thank-you note) that would actually shift their experience?
